data.title: Meta hires two Apple AI researchers for Superintelligence push, Bloomberg News reports
data.description:
Meta Platforms has hired Apple artificial intelligence researchers Mark Lee and Tom Gunter for its Superintelligence Labs team, Bloomberg News reported on Thursday, as it intensifies its pursuit for top AI talent.

The social media giant is among several technology companies that have struck high-profile deals and doled out multi-million-dollar pay packages to fast-track development of machines that could surpass humans intelligence.

Lee recently left Apple and has started at Meta, while Gunter is set to join soon, the report said, citing people familiar with the matter.

Their exit follows Ruoming Pang, who previously left Apple for a multi-million-dollar compensation package at Meta, according to a media report earlier this month.

Lee and Gunter both worked closely with Pang, the Bloomberg report said on Thursday.

Pang was the head of Apple's Foundation Models team and responsible for advanced AI features, sources have told Reuters.

A Meta spokesperson declined to comment on the report, while Apple did not immediately respond to a Reuters request for comment.

Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg said on Monday that his social media platform would spend hundreds of billions of dollars to build several massive AI data centers.

In recent weeks, Zuckerberg has personally led an aggressive talent raid in the pursuit to create a division called Superintelligence Labs.

data.title: HSBC Buys Back Nearly 4.3 Million Shares From Morgan Stanley
data.description:
HSBC bought back for cancellation nearly 4.3 million shares from Morgan Stanley on Thursday, according to a Hong Kong bourse filing Friday.

The bank bought 2,507,150 shares from UK avenues, which include the London Stock Exchange, Aquis Exchange, and Cboe Europe, at a volume-weighted average price of 9.29 pounds sterling each.

HSBC also repurchased a further 1,792,000 shares from the Hong Kong Stock Exchange at a volume-weighted average price of HK$97.84 apiece.

The shares were repurchased as part of the company's buyback program.

data.title: Superloop Guidance Probably Won't Come Until October — Market Talk
data.description: Investors shouldn't expect any formal guidance from Superloop when the fixed-line telecommunications provider reports its fiscal 2025 results, Jarden analysts warn. They expect underlying operating momentum to continue into fiscal 2026, but don't anticipate any formal guidance until October's annual general meeting. Any outlook commentary at that point is likely to be conservative, they reckon. The Jarden analysts point out in a note to clients that Superloop has a track record of beating its upfront estimates, but caution that subscriber growth from its partnership with Origin Energy is out of its control. Jarden raises its target price 3.3% to A$3.10 and stays overweight on the stock, which is up 2.4% at A$3.02. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

data.title: Andean Silver Raises AU$30 Million via Share Placement; Shares Fall 7%
data.description:
Andean Silver secured commitments to raise AU$30 million through the placing of 25 million shares at AU$1.20 apiece, according to a Friday filing with the Australian bourse.

Proceeds will be used for drilling to increase mineral resource estimates in brownfield and greenfield areas, and convert inferred resources into measured and indicated resources, the filing said.

Shares fell 7% in morning trade on Friday.

Redeye: Enea - Valuation gap supports continued upside
data.description:
Redeye believes Enea’s share price has further to run following the positive market reaction to its Q2 2025 earnings. Enea reported a solid quarter, with flat year-on-year net sales of SEK234.9m in local currencies and an adjusted EBITDAC margin of 22%, supported by a sharp reversal in OPEX that demonstrated financial discipline. Trading at an undemanding 8.0x EV/FCF for 2026e, the company’s valuation remains compelling. Redeye sees Enea’s buyback policy and improving prospects—underpinned by guidance for growth in 2025—as the key factors supporting a re-rating of the share.

data.title: Yen Set to Stay Under Pressure in Near Term, TD Securities Says — Market Talk
data.description: The yen is poised to stay under pressure in the near term, TD Securities strategists say in a research report. Surveys suggest Japan's ruling coalition won't be able to win over 50 seats in Sunday's Upper House elections to keep its simple majority, the strategists say. FX options indicate rising bearishness toward JPY as markets price in election and fiscal deterioration risks, the strategists note. "If PM Ishiba decides to resign on an election loss, USD/JPY could easily break above 149.70 as it would usher in an initial period of political turbulence," the strategists add. USD/JPY is 0.1% lower at 148.50. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: The Global Risks That Come With the Loss of an Independent Fed — WSJ
data.description:
By David Uberti and Justin Lahart

President Trump's threat to attempt to fire Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell has raised a pressing but potentially unanswerable question: What would the global economy and financial markets look like without an independent U.S. central bank?

Shielded from White House interference, the independent Fed has increasingly served as an anchor for U.S. and global markets, stepping in to steady the ship during the 2008-09 financial crisis, the Covid pandemic and other shocks of recent years. Economists credit the central bank's ability to help keep things stable in large part to its power to make moves it deems necessary, regardless of politics.

Now, former Fed officials and investors warn that a central bank more beholden to the White House could lose its ability to act quickly and credibly in the face of financial threats. On Tuesday, Trump suggested in a White House meeting with Republican lawmakers that he could soon try to oust Powell, escalating months' worth of criticism that the central bank has kept interest rates too high for too long. The president later said it was highly unlikely that he would fire Powell.

"Politicians almost always think interest rates should be lower than they are," said Alan Blinder, a Princeton University economist and former Fed vice chair. "This is the major reason we need an independent central bank."

The possibility that the firewall could crumble holds the potential to reverberate across markets at an unpredictable moment for the economy. The U.S. is still shaking off years of inflation above the Fed's longstanding 2% target, with recent data showing that price pressures from Trump's trade wars are beginning to mount. Central bankers and investors the world over are waiting on the Fed's next move.

Economists fear that an unprecedented shake-up at the center of it all could add to the uncertainty — and undermine the Fed's credibility when the next crisis explodes. That could matter not just for the U.S. economy, but other countries' economies, too. When markets around the world have spiraled lower in the past, such as during the 1998 Russian debt crisis, the Fed has stepped in to shore them up.

The Fed's ability to do so relies in part on its capacity to keep credit flowing when markets are freezing up, according to Harvard University economist Jeremy Stein. That requires that thousands of Fed employees are able to quickly step into the breach. He worries that threats to the Fed's independence will over time erode the talent and depth of experience of the Fed staff.

"That — and of course the quality of leadership — can potentially make a huge difference in a crisis," said Stein, a governor at the central bank from 2012 to 2014.

The Fed has frequently participated in international financial rescues, such as of Mexico in 1982 and again in 1995, often to prevent a chain reaction of panic from destabilizing the global financial system. It maintains "swap" lines under which it lends dollars to foreign central banks who then lend the dollars to their own banks when they are under stress. The Fed worked with global central banks and finance ministers in the financial crisis to stop big financial firms from failing.

The Fed typically worked with the administration of the day on these rescues. It is unclear if a Fed more directly controlled by Trump would participate as readily in such efforts, or attach different conditions related to Trump's other priorities.

The tension flaring now during a period of relative calm revolves around the core of Trump's agenda — tariffs — and the extent to which they could spur knock-on inflation in earnest.

Trump's pressure campaign for rate cuts has played out in public in recent months, with the president at times mocking Powell as "Mr. Too Late." After federal data Tuesday suggested inflation accelerated modestly in June — due in part to tariff impacts — Trump said the central bank should slash its roughly 4.3% federal-funds rate by 3 percentage points.

"You say that to any economist, and they don't know whether to laugh or cry," said Blinder, who served at the central bank from 1994 to 1996.

Low interest rates encourage businesses and consumers to borrow and invest more, which can boost economic growth. They also risk pushing inflation substantially higher. "If you're unwilling to raise interest rates to fight inflation, inflation is going to win," Blinder said.

Low rates can also inflate financial markets. Investors, unable to generate strong returns in safe assets, such as short-term Treasury notes, will move into progressively higher-risk assets, where returns are higher, but so is the danger of steep losses.

One criticism of the Fed following the 2008-09 financial crisis was that it wasn't mindful enough of how dangerous the bubble in housing prices and mortgage-related assets had become. Not everybody agrees that the Fed was too complacent back then, but a politicized Fed might be more apt to look the other way as financial excess built up.

Another worry is that when the Fed does step in to help markets, it only emboldens investors to take even more risk later, on the assumption the central bank will bail them out if trouble hits.

While low rates can fuel an asset bubble, they can also make it more difficult for the Fed to right the economy after a bubble bursts, notes Robert Barbera, director of the Center for Financial Economics at Johns Hopkins University. That is because with rates already low, the central bank would have less scope for cutting them further.

"The room to deliver largesse will greatly shrink if you're in the largesse-provision business all of the time," he said.

The Fed sets a short-term lending rate called the federal-funds rate. Trump has argued that the Fed should lower rates because it could help reduce interest payments on the U.S. government's massive pile of debt.

But the short-term borrowing costs controlled by the Fed don't necessarily dictate rates for longer-term loans, like mortgages, offered by commercial banks. Those rates reflect where investors believe the Fed will be setting rates well into the future. They could go substantially higher if they think today's Fed is being pressured into causing tomorrow's inflation problem.

While the Fed has cut its borrowing rate by about 1 percentage point over the past year, the average U.S. mortgage rate has held roughly steady, according to Freddie Mac.

"If you're forcing the central bank to cut short-term interest rates, and it's pretty clear to markets that's the case, then it may well be that long-term interest rates go up in the opposite direction, and in a big way," said Raghuram Rajan, an economist at the University of Chicago Booth School of Business who was governor of the Reserve Bank of India from 2013 to 2016.

This dynamic played out Wednesday immediately after reports of Trump's threat, with investors selling off long-dated Treasurys. The U.S. dollar and stock market weakened, while the price of gold — seen as a safe haven in times of financial stress — rallied.

Those moves moderated after Trump denied planning to fire Powell. "I don't rule out anything," he told reporters at the White House. "But I think it's highly unlikely."

Still, investors fear that volatility could be a preview of markets' reaction if Trump ends up following through on his threat. Goldman Sachs Chief Executive David Solomon said Wednesday that Fed independence is "something we should fight to preserve."

"I think central bank independence, not just here in the United States but around the world, has served us incredibly well," he told CNBC. Other bank CEOs echoed the sentiment.

Some Fed skeptics question how independent the central bank actually is anyway, since it works alongside the White House in times of crisis. Others question that independence because it may constrain a president's agenda to increase spending, cut taxes or impose tariffs.

Oren Cass, founder of the right-leaning think tank American Compass, argued that keeping rates elevated would subvert Trump's attempt to boost manufacturing, even if tariffs buoy some prices.

Writing on Substack this week, Cass called the Fed's approach "a nakedly political decision intended to frustrate legitimate economic policy."

Write to David Uberti at david.uberti@wsj.com and Justin Lahart at Justin.Lahart@wsj.com

data.title: Surplus supply, tepid demand push India rates to over two-year low
data.description:
Key points:
  • Thai rates widen on increase in production costs
  • Summer-autumn harvest in Mekong Delta peaking, trader says
  • Bangladesh to import rice ahead of upcoming flood season

Indian rice prices fell to their lowest level in more than two years on subdued demand and surplus supplies, while Vietnamese rice exports to Africa and the Philippines remained steady.

India's 5% broken parboiled variety (RI-INBKN5-P1) was quoted at $377-$382 per ton, its lowest since early December 2022, and down from last week’s $380-$385.

Indian 5% broken white rice was priced at $373 to $378 per ton this week.

"Overseas buyers have multiple options, everyone from Thailand, Vietnam, to India wants to sell their excess stocks, so buyers are trying hard to negotiate a lower price," said a New Delhi-based trader with a global trade house.

Vietnam's 5% broken rice (RI-VNBKN5-P1) was offered at $380-$390 per metric ton, traders said. The price was $382 a week ago, according to the Vietnam Food Association.

"Demand from the Philippines and Africa remains steady," a trader based in Ho Chi Minh City said.

Preliminary shipping data showed 256,583 tons of rice is to be loaded at Ho Chi Minh City port in July, with most of the rice heading to the Philippines and Malaysia.

Traders said the summer-autumn harvest in the Mekong Delta, the country's key rice growing area, was peaking.

Thailand's 5% broken rice (RI-THBKN5-P1) was quoted at $380-$385 per tonne, compared with last week's $380.

Traders said prices slightly widened over the week due to an increase in the cost of rice production in the domestic market as international demand remains quiet.

A Bangkok-based trader said new supply of rice will likely enter the market at the end of this month and early next month.

"Many buyers are waiting for the new crop which could drive the price down," the trader added.

Meanwhile, Bangladesh will import 400,000 metric tons of rice to strengthen food security ahead of the flood season, food ministry officials said.

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

2130 ET - The yen is poised to stay under pressure in the near term, TD Securities strategists say in a research report. Surveys suggest Japan's ruling coalition won't be able to win over 50 seats in Sunday's Upper House elections to keep its simple majority, the strategists say. FX options indicate rising bearishness toward JPY as markets price in election and fiscal deterioration risks, the strategists note. "If PM Ishiba decides to resign on an election loss, USD/JPY could easily break above 149.70 as it would usher in an initial period of political turbulence," the strategists add. USD/JPY is 0.1% lower at 148.50. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2101 ET - The Philippine central bank is expected to deliver three rate cuts by year-end, J.P. Morgan economists write in a note. The impact of U.S. tariffs is limited given the country's small trade exposure, they say. Trump has recently raised the Philippine's tariff rate to 20% from 17%. Domestic demand remains weak, the economists say, and the BSP's rate cuts so far has been unable to revive credit demand due to factors such as supply overhang in the residential property market. Inflation is also likely to remain below-target through 1Q 2026.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

2034 ET - Asian currencies consolidate against the dollar in the early Asian session amid mixed signals. On the one hand, stronger-than-expected U.S. economic data released overnight could dent Fed rate-cut expectations, thereby supporting the greenback. However, "USD remains vulnerable to the downside if concerns about U.S. policymaking further undermine investor confidence in USD assets," CBA's Carol Kong says in a research report. The dollar's weakness earlier this week due to worries over Fed Chair Powell's possible dismissal by President Trump is a "case in point," the economist and currency strategist says. USD/CNH is little changed at 7.1823; USD/TWD is steady at 29.43. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2017 ET - Japan's underlying inflation is still elevated, and is almost certain to overshoot BOJ's forecasts, Capital Economics' Abhijit Surya says in commentary. The country's inflation excluding fresh food and energy rose 3.4% on year in June, up from the 3.3% increase in May, the senior APAC economist notes. "There continues to be a compelling case for the BOJ to resume its tightening cycle later this year," Surya says. However, U.S.-Japan tariff negotiations remain "up in the air," so there's a growing risk that the BOJ will delay its next rate increase until early 2026, Surya adds. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2014 ET - Japanese stocks are higher following signs of U.S. economic strength. Tech and financial stocks are leading gains. Nomura Research Institute is up 1.0% and Sumitomo Mitsui Financial Group is 0.6% higher. USD/JPY is at 148.47, compared with 148.73 as of Thursday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are focusing on any developments related to U.S. tariffs and domestic economic policy ahead of the Upper House elections on Sunday. The Nikkei Stock Average is up 0.2% at 39968.38. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

2010 ET - JGBs consolidate ahead of Japan's Upper House elections on Sunday. Surveys surrounding the elections show prospects of the Liberal Democratic Party-Komeito coalition retaining its majority have grown even more uncertain than earlier in the campaign, two members of Barclays' FICC Research say in a research report. "If the fiscally dovish opposition parties fare even better than projected in the pre-election polls, the initial post-election response would likely be an accelerated bear-steepening in JGBs," the members say. The 10-year JGB yield is steady at 1.555%. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1942 ET - Japanese stocks may rise following signs of U.S. economic strength. Nikkei futures are up 0.4% at 40050 on the SGX. USD/JPY is at 148.31, compared with 148.73 as of Thursday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are focusing on any developments related to U.S. tariffs and domestic economic policy ahead of the Upper House election on Sunday. The Nikkei Stock Average rose 0.6% to 39901.19 on Thursday. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com)

1921 ET - Asked in an audience Q&A in Manhattan whether Fed governors are committed to maintaining their independence from political leaders, Fed Gov. Christopher Waller gives a strong affirmative. "I've never met a central banker in my life who didn't cherish it," Waller says of the principle that monetary authorities should get a free hand. Harsh criticism of the Fed from the White House has made central-bank independence a hot-button topic. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1904 ET - Fed governor Christopher Waller swiftly denies that anyone from the Trump administration has talked to him about succeeding Fed Chair Jerome Powell. Waller is speaking before a group of finance pros in Manhattan, laying out the case that the Fed should cut rates in July. In an audience Q&A, an attendee asks him if the Trump administration has approached him about Powell's job. "Nope!" he responds, and moves on to the next question. (matt.grossman@wsj.com; @mattgrossman)

1758 ET - Netflix isn't seeing any pain points in the economy from its end. Executives on the streaming service's 2Q investor call said while they're watching consumer sentiment in the broader economy, at this time there's nothing significant to note in the metrics and indicators it gets through its business. This comes as Netflix has cracked down on password sharing, built out an ad-supported offering and raised prices on some subscription tiers. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1745 ET - The venture capital industry's main trade group saluted the House passage of three cryptocurrency-related bills. "These bills will enhance the venture industry's ability to back transformative companies and drive lasting economic growth," said Bobby Franklin, president and CEO of National Venture Capital Association. He said the bills, GENIUS Act, the CLARITY Act, and the Anti-CBDC Surveillance State Act, establish a clear regulatory framework for stablecoins and other digital assets. Crypto startups globally raised $4.9 billion via 446 venture-capital deals in the first quarter of this year, according to research from Galaxy. Of those deals about 39% were for companies based in the U.S. ( yuliya.chernova@wsj.com )

1713 ET - U.S. home sales rose in June from May, marking five consecutive months of gains, RE/MAX Holdings reports. Chicago had the highest number of sales in June, jumping 12.9% from the same time last year, the report says. Buyers and sellers in the windy city's market have finally come to terms with the current housing landscape, RE/MAX premier broker Mike Opyd says. The mental shift has pushed more sellers to offer properties on the market for buyers to jump on before prices rise even further, Opyd says. (natalie.weger@wsj.com; @NatalieWeger)

data.title: Redeye: Ependion - Order bookings picking up
data.description:
Redeye remains confident on the case and leave our forecasts and valuation virtually unchanged following the Q2 report. Sales and earnings were basically as expected while order bookings improved across the board. Ependion’s near-term outlook remains cautious, but we sense a bit more optimism as business activity is high and the Group is exposed to several markets with solid underlying structural growth.

Read more and download the Research Update.

data.title: Palm Oil Demand Set to Rise as Indonesia Eyes Biodiesel Mandate Rollout — Market Talk
data.description: Palm oil demand could get a further boost if Indonesia proceeds with its B50 biodiesel plan, say CIMB analysts Ivy Ng Lee Fang and Lim Yue Jia. The current B40 policy--which mandates a 40% palm-based biodiesel blend--is expected to boost domestic palm oil consumption by 2 million tons and reduce its exportable surplus. A potential shift to B50 in 2026 could raise domestic palm oil use by 3 million tons, they say. This would help offset pressures from U.S. tariffs and support crude palm oil prices, they add. CIMB maintains its 2025 CPO price forecast at 4,200 ringgit/ton and continues to prefer IOI Corp. and Hap Seng Plantations as the plantation sector's top picks. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

data.title: Aussie Broadband Could Benefit From Elevated Churn — Market Talk
data.description: The remainder of 2025 is seen by Jarden analysts as a pivotal period for Aussie Broadband to potentially grab market share from rival providers. They flag the impending acceleration of download and upload speeds on Australia's national broadband network as a key event likely to drive elevated levels of customer churn across the fixed-line communications industry. This looks like an opportunity for challengers such as Aussie Broadband, they write in a note to clients. However, they think that competition appears to be ramping up and lack sufficient conviction to raise their recommendation to buy. Jarden raises its target price 3.4% to A$4.50 and stays overweight on the stock. Shares are down 0.2% at A$4.13. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

data.title: Update: Qantas Airways Faces Class Action Over Customer Data Breach
data.description:
(Updates to add Qantas' response to MT Newswires' request for comment in the third paragraph and the company's statement in the last paragraph)

Qantas Airways is facing a class action lawsuit filed by Maurice Blackburn with the Office of the Australian Information Commissioner, the law firm's website showed.

The class action alleges that the airline breached privacy laws by failing to safeguard the data of millions of customers, including contact details, birth dates, and Frequent Flyer numbers.

In response to a request for comment from MT Newswires, a Qantas spokesperson acknowledged the complaint, adding that the company has secured a New South Wales Supreme Court injunction to block access to, use of, or publication of the stolen data.

In a Thursday statement, the company said that there continues to be no evidence that any of the data stolen had been released, adding that passwords, personal identification numbers, and log-in details were not compromised.

data.title: Almond Price Impact on Select Harvests Is Hard to Quantify — Market Talk
data.description: Wilsons analysts say it will be hard to quantify the impact on Select Harvests of recent almond price weakness on the nut farmer. With the Australia-listed company opting against reaffirming annual price guidance, analyst James Ferrier and Declan Carroll say that sharp drops in spot almond prices over the past week introduce earnings risk. However, they tell clients in a note that price volatility and differences across variety and grade make it hard to nail down the impact. They maintain a market-weight recommendation for Select Harvests for now. Wilsons cuts its target price 17% to A$4.02. Shares are down 3.3% at A$3.79. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

data.title: Mesoblast Reports Over $13 Million in Gross Revenue From Ryoncil Product Sales; Shares Soar 25%
data.description:
Mesoblast reported $13.2 million in unaudited gross revenue from sales of its Ryoncil mesenchymal stromal cell product in the June quarter after its launch on March 28, according to a Friday Australian bourse filing.

The firm earlier secured the US Food and Drug Administration's approval for Ryoncil for the treatment of steroid-refractory acute graft-versus-host disease in children.

The firm said it has onboarded over 25 transplant centers since the launch of Ryoncil, and it expects to complete the onboarding process across all 45 priority transplant centers in the September quarter.

The drugmaker also reported $1.6 million in revenue from royalties on the sales of Temcell HS Inj. sold in Japan by its licensee.

Mesoblast's shares soared 25% in early trading on Friday.

data.title: TSMC jumps after stellar second-quarter results
data.description:
** Taipei-listed shares of TSMC opens more than 2% higher, a day after reporting record-breaking second quarter net profit that easily beat market expectations on Thursday

** Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co (TSMC) is the world's main producer of advanced AI chips, whose major clients include Apple and Nvidia

** Benchmark Taiwan SE weighted Index TWSE:TAIEX up 1.5%

** TSMC's shares surged some 80% last year but have climbed just 7% for the year to date on worries about tariffs and unfavourable currency exchange rates

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

2110 ET - Central Plaza Hotel is likely to enter a new earnings growth cycle from 2026, Thanachart Securities' Siriporn Arunothai says in a research report. Main drivers are improvements in the Thai company's hotel and food businesses, the analyst says. Also, its hotel business in Japan continues to improve, while its hotel business in the Maldives is expected to improve following the opening of a new terminal at the international airport this month. However, the brokerage trims its 2025-2027 earnings estimates for the company by 0.1%-3.0% to reflect a more conservative outlook on international tourist arrivals in Thailand. It lowers the stock's target price to THB35.00 from THB37.00 with an unchanged buy rating. Shares last closed at THB27.00. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2108 ET - Sunway's latest 3.91-acre land acquisition in Chuan Grove in Singapore appears positive, given its strategic location, Maybank IB analyst Wong Wei Sum says in a note. The deal marks its first partnership with Sing Holdings Residential and expands its footprint in a prime location, she says. Assuming a 12% margin over five years, the Singapore project could lift EPS by about MYR0.003 from 2027, she adds. However, Maybank downgrades Sunway's rating to hold from buy following its recent 14% share price rally and maintains a MYR5.31 target price. Shares are 0.8% higher at MYR5.03. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

2102 ET - CAR Group's leadership change is seen by Morgans analyst Steven Sassine as a demonstration of the bench strength and quality of management developed under its departing chief executive. Sassine concedes that CEO Cameron McIntyre's decision to step down is a loss to the Australia-listed vehicle advertiser, but is nonetheless positive on the elevation of CFO William Elliott as his successor. He marginally lowers his revenue and earnings forecasts for the next three years, but the changes are linked to CAR's unaudited fiscal 2025 results rather than the management changes. Morgans keeps an accumulate rating and A$40.80 target price on the stock, which is up 1.9% at A$37.025. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

2059 ET - Singapore semiconductor-components maker UMS Integration could see multiple valuation re-ratings, UOB Kay Hian's John Cheong says in a note. Cheong expects UMS to meet its 2Q earnings guidance of S$11 million, thanks to healthy orders from a new customer and after resolving 1Q supply disruption issues. The company's dual listing in Bursa Malaysia, commencing on August 1, has received strong investor interest. UMS, trading at around a 25% discount versus its Malaysian peers, expects to narrow the valuation gap with its peers after the dual listing, Cheong notes. UOB KH increases the stock's target to S$1.73 from S$1.32 to reflect valuation re-ratings from the dual listing and better earnings quality, and maintains a buy rating. Shares last closed 1.4% higher at S$1.43. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

2041 ET - ResMed's bulls at Jarden see potential for the breathing-tech maker's 4Q gross margin to surprise to the upside. Analysts Steve Wheen and Tristan Maher forecast on-quarter margin expansion of 20 bps, but tell clients in a note that an even greater improvement is possible. They cite manufacturing and distribution efficiencies, product mix, scale advantages and currency tailwinds as reasons for their optimism. While their 60.1% margin forecast is in line with consensus, they observe that a result of 60.5% would lift EPS by 1.3% relative to their forecasts. Jarden has an overweight rating and A$40.55 target price on the stock, which is down 0.1% at A$39.48. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

2017 ET - Hankook Tire & Technology will likely be able to offset U.S. tariff costs by raising product prices, Samsung Securities analysts Esther Yim and Hyunzi Kim write in a note. The South Korean tire maker raised prices for its tire products by 5%-8% in the U.S. in July after increasing them by 2%-4% each in Europe and Korea earlier this year, they say. In response to Trump's higher duties, the company is boosting production at its plants in Tennessee, with a new factory set to begin operations in 3Q, the analysts note. They are upbeat on Hankook's improving shareholder returns, with its dividend yield expected to rise to 6.1% this year, up from 5.2% in 2024. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

2014 ET - Japanese stocks are higher following signs of U.S. economic strength. Tech and financial stocks are leading gains. Nomura Research Institute is up 1.0% and Sumitomo Mitsui Financial Group is 0.6% higher. USD/JPY is at 148.47, compared with 148.73 as of Thursday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are focusing on any developments related to U.S. tariffs and domestic economic policy ahead of the Upper House elections on Sunday. The Nikkei Stock Average is up 0.2% at 39968.38. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

1942 ET - Japanese stocks may rise following signs of U.S. economic strength. Nikkei futures are up 0.4% at 40050 on the SGX. USD/JPY is at 148.31, compared with 148.73 as of Thursday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are focusing on any developments related to U.S. tariffs and domestic economic policy ahead of the Upper House election on Sunday. The Nikkei Stock Average rose 0.6% to 39901.19 on Thursday. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com)

1935 ET - Australia's S&P/ASX 200 looks set to round out the week on a high after U.S. indices closed at fresh records. ASX futures are up by 0.6% ahead of Friday's session, suggesting that the benchmark index, which closed Thursday at a record, will add to the 0.7% rise compiled so far this week. Ahead of the open, mining giant BHP reported higher annual iron-ore and copper output, but flagged higher-than-expected costs at its giant Canadian potash operation. The S&P 500 and Nasdaq Composite closed at records after upbeat earnings from some major companies. The S&P added 0.5% and the Nasdaq gained 0.7%. The DJIA rose 0.5%. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

1830 ET - Netflix still trusts its playbook. Executives on the streaming service's 2Q investor call note that consolidation of studio and network assets is likely to continue, but they don't think it materially changes the competitive landscape. As for whether they will partake in such consolidation, the executives note they've historically built their own capabilities rather than buy them and believe there is a lot of runway for growth without fundamentally changing that playbook. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1820 ET - Netflix faces competition from a number of platforms for time spent watching content, but it also knows there's a lot of market share left to grab. Executives on the streaming service's 2Q investor call note competition for share of TV time is stiff between traditional cable, other streamers, video games and social media. But they also note that there's about 8% of total TV viewership that neither Netflix or Youtube are winning right now, and that's where the vast majority of its time and money is focused. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

1804 ET - Netflix's redesigned user interface was meant to reflect where its platform is today. Executives on the streaming service's 2Q investor call said the previous UI was designed for the Netflix of 10 years ago. The company has drastically evolved since, notably in areas including live events and games. Executives pointed to the fact that last week the platform for the first time held a concurrent pair of live events, a significant step up from its initial foray into live streams that were sometimes marred by glitches and lagging streams. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

data.title: Central Plaza Hotel Likely to Enter New Earnings Growth Cycle — Market Talk
data.description: Central Plaza Hotel is likely to enter a new earnings growth cycle from 2026, Thanachart Securities' Siriporn Arunothai says in a research report. Main drivers are improvements in the Thai company's hotel and food businesses, the analyst says. Also, its hotel business in Japan continues to improve, while its hotel business in the Maldives is expected to improve following the opening of a new terminal at the international airport this month. However, the brokerage trims its 2025-2027 earnings estimates for the company by 0.1%-3.0% to reflect a more conservative outlook on international tourist arrivals in Thailand. It lowers the stock's target price to THB35.00 from THB37.00 with an unchanged buy rating. Shares last closed at THB27.00. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)
